title: BFI 75 Most Wanted

infobox.value: Year | Title | Director | Notes

1926 | *[The Mountain Eagle](/wiki/The_Mountain_Eagle "The Mountain Eagle")* | [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") | The only lost Hitchcock feature film (his short *[An Elastic Affair](/wiki/An_Elastic_Affair "An Elastic Affair")* is also lost). One of the world's most sought-after lost films.

1931 | *[Two Crowded Hours](/wiki/Two_Crowded_Hours "Two Crowded Hours")* | [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell_(director) "Michael Powell (director)") | Powell's directorial debut, an unexpected box-office success

1943 | *[Squadron Leader X](/wiki/Squadron_Leader_X "Squadron Leader X")* | [Lance Comfort](/wiki/Lance_Comfort "Lance Comfort") | Extremely well-reviewed at the time of release, sought due to critical reassessment of Comfort's importance in British cinema history. Story by [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger").

1968 | *[Sleep Is Lovely](/wiki/Sleep_Is_Lovely "Sleep Is Lovely")* (aka, *The Other People*) | [David Hart](/wiki/David_Hart_(UK_political_activist) "David Hart (UK political activist)") | Believed to be experimental in filming style, no evidence of screening to a trade or paying audience.

1973 | *[Symptoms](/wiki/Symptoms_(film) "Symptoms (film)")* | [José Ramón Larraz](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ram%C3%B3n_Larraz "José Ramón Larraz") | British entry in the [1974 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1974_Cannes_Film_Festival "1974 Cannes Film Festival"). Also believed to circulate privately through bootlegs, but for many years the negatives remained missing. The film was obtained by February 2016 and has since been released on DVD.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

1948 | *[Somewhere in Politics](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Politics "Somewhere in Politics")* | [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley") | [Mancunian Films](/wiki/Mancunian_Films "Mancunian Films") production starring [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle"). An 18-minute segment survives.

1929 | *[The Last Post](/wiki/The_Last_Post_(film) "The Last Post (film)")* | [Dinah Shurey](/wiki/Dinah_Shurey "Dinah Shurey") | Solo directorial debut of Britain's only female film director of this period.

1960 | *[Linda](/wiki/Linda_(1960_film) "Linda (1960 film)")* | [Don Sharp](/wiki/Don_Sharp "Don Sharp") | Teen-drama starring [Carol White](/wiki/Carol_White "Carol White") and [Alan Rothwell](/wiki/Alan_Rothwell "Alan Rothwell"). Originally shown on a double-bill with *[Saturday Night and Sunday Morning](/wiki/Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film) "Saturday Night and Sunday Morning (film)")*, the film has been rediscovered and was shown on [Talking Pictures TV](/wiki/Talking_Pictures_TV "Talking Pictures TV") in August 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

1914 | *[A Study in Scarlet](/wiki/A_Study_in_Scarlet_(1914_British_film) "A Study in Scarlet (1914 British film)")* | [George Pearson](/wiki/George_Pearson_(filmmaker) "George Pearson (filmmaker)") | Earliest British Sherlock Holmes feature

1913 | *[Maria Marten, or the Mystery of the Red Barn](/wiki/Maria_Marten,_or_the_Mystery_of_the_Red_Barn "Maria Marten, or the Mystery of the Red Barn")* | [Maurice Elvey](/wiki/Maurice_Elvey "Maurice Elvey") | From first year of Elvey's directorial career. Dramatisation of the notorious [Red Barn murder](/wiki/Red_Barn_murder "Red Barn murder"), filmed in the actual locations in which the events took place
Title | Director | Notes
*[Milestones](/wiki/Milestones_(1916_film) "Milestones (1916 film)")* | [Thomas Bentley](/wiki/Thomas_Bentley_(director) "Thomas Bentley (director)") | Ambitious multi-generational family saga

*[The First Men in the Moon](/wiki/The_First_Men_in_the_Moon_(1919_film) "The First Men in the Moon (1919 film)")* | J. L. V. Leigh | First direct [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") film adaptation
*[The Amazing Quest of Mr. Ernest Bliss](/wiki/The_Amazing_Quest_of_Mr._Ernest_Bliss "The Amazing Quest of Mr. Ernest Bliss")* | [Henry Edwards](/wiki/Henry_Edwards_(actor) "Henry Edwards (actor)") |
*[The Adventures of Mr. Pickwick](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Mr._Pickwick "The Adventures of Mr. Pickwick")* | Thomas Bentley | Early [Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") adaptation

*[Love, Life and Laughter](/wiki/Love,_Life_and_Laughter_(1923_film) "Love, Life and Laughter (1923 film)")* | George Pearson | Acclaimed on release as "a screen classic" and "a masterpiece". On 2 April 2014 Dutch filmmuseum EYE reported that it had discovered a copy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Now in the BFI National Archive.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
*[Lily of the Alley](/wiki/Lily_of_the_Alley "Lily of the Alley")* | Henry Edwards | Experimental silent without use of [intertitles](/wiki/Intertitles "Intertitles")

*[London](/wiki/London_(1926_film) "London (1926 film)")* | [Herbert Wilcox](/wiki/Herbert_Wilcox "Herbert Wilcox") | Big-budget "Limehouse" picture starring [Dorothy Gish](/wiki/Dorothy_Gish "Dorothy Gish")

*[The Arcadians](/wiki/The_Arcadians_(film) "The Arcadians (film)")* | [Victor Saville](/wiki/Victor_Saville "Victor Saville") | Curiosity as to how a silent version was made of a popular stage musical

*[The Crooked Billet](/wiki/The_Crooked_Billet "The Crooked Billet")* | [Adrian Brunel](/wiki/Adrian_Brunel "Adrian Brunel") | Starring [Madeleine Carroll](/wiki/Madeleine_Carroll "Madeleine Carroll"). May have been released in both silent and sound versions
*[Lord Richard in the Pantry](/wiki/Lord_Richard_in_the_Pantry "Lord Richard in the Pantry")* | [Walter Forde](/wiki/Walter_Forde "Walter Forde") |
*[Deadlock](/wiki/Deadlock_(1931_film) "Deadlock (1931 film)")* | George King | First British talkie to use a film set as its dramatic location.

*[Castle Sinister](/wiki/Castle_Sinister "Castle Sinister")* | [Widgey R. Newman](/wiki/Widgey_R._Newman "Widgey R. Newman") | Early British horror film, intriguing tagline "Mad doctor tries to put girl's brain into apeman's head"
*[Counsel's Opinion](/wiki/Counsel%27s_Opinion "Counsel's Opinion")* | [Allan Dwan](/wiki/Allan_Dwan "Allan Dwan") | Early [Alexander Korda](/wiki/Alexander_Korda "Alexander Korda") production

is a list compiled in 2010 by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") of the most sought-after British feature films not held in the [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive"), and classified as "missing, believed [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film")". The films chosen range from [quota quickies](/wiki/Quota_quickies "Quota quickies") and [B-movies](/wiki/B-movies "B-movies") to lavish prestige productions of their day. The list includes lost works by major directors and those featuring top-name actors; also films that were top box-office successes in their time but have since disappeared, and works that are believed to be historically significant for some aspect of style, technique, subject matter or innovation.[[1]](#cite_note-BFI75-1)

first_paragraph: The **BFI 75 Most Wanted** is a list compiled in 2010 by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") of the most sought-after British feature films not held in the [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive"), and classified as "missing, believed [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film")". The films chosen range from [quota quickies](/wiki/Quota_quickies "Quota quickies") and [B-movies](/wiki/B-movies "B-movies") to lavish prestige productions of their day. The list includes lost works by major directors and those featuring top-name actors; also films that were top box-office successes in their time but have since disappeared, and works that are believed to be historically significant for some aspect of style, technique, subject matter or innovation.[[1]](#cite_note-BFI75-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

List of films

The **BFI 75 Most Wanted** is a list compiled in 2010 by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") of the most sought-after British feature films not held in the [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive"), and classified as "missing, believed [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film")". The films chosen range from [quota quickies](/wiki/Quota_quickies "Quota quickies") and [B-movies](/wiki/B-movies "B-movies") to lavish prestige productions of their day. The list includes lost works by major directors and those featuring top-name actors; also films that were top box-office successes in their time but have since disappeared, and works that are believed to be historically significant for some aspect of style, technique, subject matter or innovation.[[1]](#cite_note-BFI75-1)

The earliest film on the list dates from 1913, the latest from 1983. The 1930s is the most represented decade with 24 entries, followed by the 1920s (16), and the 1940s (14). [Maurice Elvey](/wiki/Maurice_Elvey "Maurice Elvey"), with four films on the list, is the most represented director. The first film on the list is [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s 1926 feature *[The Mountain Eagle](/wiki/The_Mountain_Eagle "The Mountain Eagle")*, described as "the Holy Grail of film historians".[[1]](#cite_note-BFI75-1)

Since 2012, the BFI has revealed that a number of the films on the list have been found.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) As of 2017, 18 of the 75 films have been found in their complete form; two others exist in shortened, retitled versions that were re-edited for the United States market.

## Films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BFI_75_Most_Wanted&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Films")]

The BFI 75 Most Wanted List is divided into two headings: "The Ten Most Wanted", sorted in a chosen descending order of importance, and "The Rest", in no order of importance (as published, they are listed alphabetically).[[1]](#cite_note-BFI75-1) In the following tables, the top 10 films are numbered to reflect their order in the BFI's published list, with the remaining 65 following in chronological order of release.

### The Ten Most Wanted

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BFI_75_Most_Wanted&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Ten Most Wanted")]

:   Films that have been found
:   Films that have significant segments or scenes still extant, or exist in cut versions, or in poor quality prints

| # | Year | Title | Director | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 1926 | *[The Mountain Eagle](/wiki/The_Mountain_Eagle "The Mountain Eagle")* | [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") | The only lost Hitchcock feature film (his short *[An Elastic Affair](/wiki/An_Elastic_Affair "An Elastic Affair")* is also lost). One of the world's most sought-after lost films. |
| 2 | 1931 | *[Two Crowded Hours](/wiki/Two_Crowded_Hours "Two Crowded Hours")* | [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell_(director) "Michael Powell (director)") | Powell's directorial debut, an unexpected box-office success |
| 3 | 1943 | *[Squadron Leader X](/wiki/Squadron_Leader_X "Squadron Leader X")* | [Lance Comfort](/wiki/Lance_Comfort "Lance Comfort") | Extremely well-reviewed at the time of release, sought due to critical reassessment of Comfort's importance in British cinema history. Story by [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger"). |
| 4 | 1968 | *[Sleep Is Lovely](/wiki/Sleep_Is_Lovely "Sleep Is Lovely")* (aka, *The Other People*) | [David Hart](/wiki/David_Hart_(UK_political_activist) "David Hart (UK political activist)") | Believed to be experimental in filming style, no evidence of screening to a trade or paying audience. |
| 5 | 1973 | *[Symptoms](/wiki/Symptoms_(film) "Symptoms (film)")* | [José Ramón Larraz](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ram%C3%B3n_Larraz "José Ramón Larraz") | British entry in the [1974 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1974_Cannes_Film_Festival "1974 Cannes Film Festival"). Also believed to circulate privately through bootlegs, but for many years the negatives remained missing. The film was obtained by February 2016 and has since been released on DVD.[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| 6 | 1948 | *[Somewhere in Politics](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Politics "Somewhere in Politics")* | [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley") | [Mancunian Films](/wiki/Mancunian_Films "Mancunian Films") production starring [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle"). An 18-minute segment survives. |
| 7 | 1929 | *[The Last Post](/wiki/The_Last_Post_(film) "The Last Post (film)")* | [Dinah Shurey](/wiki/Dinah_Shurey "Dinah Shurey") | Solo directorial debut of Britain's only female film director of this period. |
| 8 | 1960 | *[Linda](/wiki/Linda_(1960_film) "Linda (1960 film)")* | [Don Sharp](/wiki/Don_Sharp "Don Sharp") | Teen-drama starring [Carol White](/wiki/Carol_White "Carol White") and [Alan Rothwell](/wiki/Alan_Rothwell "Alan Rothwell"). Originally shown on a double-bill with *[Saturday Night and Sunday Morning](/wiki/Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning_(film) "Saturday Night and Sunday Morning (film)")*, the film has been rediscovered and was shown on [Talking Pictures TV](/wiki/Talking_Pictures_TV "Talking Pictures TV") in August 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-4) |
| 9 | 1914 | *[A Study in Scarlet](/wiki/A_Study_in_Scarlet_(1914_British_film) "A Study in Scarlet (1914 British film)")* | [George Pearson](/wiki/George_Pearson_(filmmaker) "George Pearson (filmmaker)") | Earliest British Sherlock Holmes feature |
| 10 | 1913 | *[Maria Marten, or the Mystery of the Red Barn](/wiki/Maria_Marten,_or_the_Mystery_of_the_Red_Barn "Maria Marten, or the Mystery of the Red Barn")* | [Maurice Elvey](/wiki/Maurice_Elvey "Maurice Elvey") | From first year of Elvey's directorial career. Dramatisation of the notorious [Red Barn murder](/wiki/Red_Barn_murder "Red Barn murder"), filmed in the actual locations in which the events took place |

### The Rest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BFI_75_Most_Wanted&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Rest")]

:   Films that have been found
:   Films that have significant segments or scenes still extant, or exist in cut versions, or in poor quality prints
:   Films that have been found and exist as complete 16mm or 35mm prints, but film prints are still missing from the BFI national archive.

| Year | Title | Director | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1916 | *[Milestones](/wiki/Milestones_(1916_film) "Milestones (1916 film)")* | [Thomas Bentley](/wiki/Thomas_Bentley_(director) "Thomas Bentley (director)") | Ambitious multi-generational family saga |
| 1919 | *[The First Men in the Moon](/wiki/The_First_Men_in_the_Moon_(1919_film) "The First Men in the Moon (1919 film)")* | J. L. V. Leigh | First direct [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") film adaptation |
| 1920 | *[The Amazing Quest of Mr. Ernest Bliss](/wiki/The_Amazing_Quest_of_Mr._Ernest_Bliss "The Amazing Quest of Mr. Ernest Bliss")* | [Henry Edwards](/wiki/Henry_Edwards_(actor) "Henry Edwards (actor)") |  |
| 1921 | *[The Adventures of Mr. Pickwick](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Mr._Pickwick "The Adventures of Mr. Pickwick")* | Thomas Bentley | Early [Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") adaptation |
| 1921 | *[The Narrow Valley](/wiki/The_Narrow_Valley "The Narrow Valley")* | [Cecil Hepworth](/wiki/Cecil_Hepworth "Cecil Hepworth") | Starring [Alma Taylor](/wiki/Alma_Taylor "Alma Taylor"), highly praised for its location shots of the [South Downs](/wiki/South_Downs "South Downs") countryside |
| 1923 | *[Love, Life and Laughter](/wiki/Love,_Life_and_Laughter_(1923_film) "Love, Life and Laughter (1923 film)")* | George Pearson | Acclaimed on release as "a screen classic" and "a masterpiece". On 2 April 2014 Dutch filmmuseum EYE reported that it had discovered a copy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Now in the BFI National Archive.[[6]](#cite_note-6) |
| 1923 | *[Reveille](/wiki/Reveille_(film) "Reveille (film)")* | George Pearson | Socially significant World War I drama. Small segments believed to survive in private hands |
| 1923 | *[Woman to Woman](/wiki/Woman_to_Woman_(1923_film) "Woman to Woman (1923 film)")* | [Graham Cutts](/wiki/Graham_Cutts "Graham Cutts") | Hitchcock as assistant director and uncredited screenwriter |
| 1924 | *[Lily of the Alley](/wiki/Lily_of_the_Alley "Lily of the Alley")* | Henry Edwards | Experimental silent without use of [intertitles](/wiki/Intertitles "Intertitles") |
| 1924 | *[Who Is the Man?](/wiki/Who_Is_the_Man%3F "Who Is the Man?")* | [Walter Summers](/wiki/Walter_Summers "Walter Summers") | Screen debut of [John Gielgud](/wiki/John_Gielgud "John Gielgud") |
| 1926 | *[London](/wiki/London_(1926_film) "London (1926 film)")* | [Herbert Wilcox](/wiki/Herbert_Wilcox "Herbert Wilcox") | Big-budget "Limehouse" picture starring [Dorothy Gish](/wiki/Dorothy_Gish "Dorothy Gish") |
| 1926 | *[Mademoiselle from Armentieres](/wiki/Mademoiselle_from_Armentieres_(film) "Mademoiselle from Armentieres (film)")* | Maurice Elvey | Highest-grossing British film of 1926. A little under one third is known to survive in fragments |
| 1927 | *[The Arcadians](/wiki/The_Arcadians_(film) "The Arcadians (film)")* | [Victor Saville](/wiki/Victor_Saville "Victor Saville") | Curiosity as to how a silent version was made of a popular stage musical |
| 1927 | *The Story of the Flag* | Anson Dyer | First full-length British animation |
| 1927 | *[Tip Toes](/wiki/Tip_Toes "Tip Toes")* | Herbert Wilcox | Another Dorothy Gish vehicle, mauled by critics |
| 1929 | *[The Crooked Billet](/wiki/The_Crooked_Billet "The Crooked Billet")* | [Adrian Brunel](/wiki/Adrian_Brunel "Adrian Brunel") | Starring [Madeleine Carroll](/wiki/Madeleine_Carroll "Madeleine Carroll"). May have been released in both silent and sound versions |
| 1930 | *[Lord Richard in the Pantry](/wiki/Lord_Richard_in_the_Pantry "Lord Richard in the Pantry")* | [Walter Forde](/wiki/Walter_Forde "Walter Forde") |  |
| 1930 | *[School for Scandal](/wiki/The_School_for_Scandal_(1930_film) "The School for Scandal (1930 film)")* | Maurice Elvey | Only film shot in the abortive Raycol colour process. Only screened in black-and-white |
| 1930 | *[Too Many Crooks](/wiki/Too_Many_Crooks_(1930_film) "Too Many Crooks (1930 film)")* | [George King](/wiki/George_King_(film_director) "George King (film director)") | British film debut of [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier") |
| 1931 | *[Deadlock](/wiki/Deadlock_(1931_film) "Deadlock (1931 film)")* | George King | First British talkie to use a film set as its dramatic location. |
| 1931 | *[Hobson's Choice](/wiki/Hobson%27s_Choice_(1931_film) "Hobson's Choice (1931 film)")* | Thomas Bentley | Conflicting reports as to whether [George Formby](/wiki/George_Formby,_Jr. "George Formby, Jr.") appeared in this film |
| 1931 | *[Lloyd of the C.I.D.](/wiki/Lloyd_of_the_C.I.D. "Lloyd of the C.I.D.")* | [Henry MacRae](/wiki/Henry_MacRae "Henry MacRae") | 12-part sound serial, the only such ever made in Britain not targeted at a juvenile audience. Known to have been extant in 1977, but has since proved untraceable |
| 1932 | *[Castle Sinister](/wiki/Castle_Sinister "Castle Sinister")* | [Widgey R. Newman](/wiki/Widgey_R._Newman "Widgey R. Newman") | Early British horror film, intriguing tagline "Mad doctor tries to put girl's brain into apeman's head" |
| 1932 | *[Men of Tomorrow](/wiki/Men_of_Tomorrow_(1932_film) "Men of Tomorrow (1932 film)")* | [Leontine Sagan](/wiki/Leontine_Sagan "Leontine Sagan") | Screen debut of [Robert Donat](/wiki/Robert_Donat "Robert Donat") |
| 1933 | *[Counsel's Opinion](/wiki/Counsel%27s_Opinion "Counsel's Opinion")* | [Allan Dwan](/wiki/Allan_Dwan "Allan Dwan") | Early [Alexander Korda](/wiki/Alexander_Korda "Alexander Korda") production |
| 1933 | *[Yes, Mr Brown](/wiki/Yes,_Mr_Brown "Yes, Mr Brown")* | [Jack Buchanan](/wiki/Jack_Buchanan "Jack Buchanan") | Buchanan's first starring and directing role |
| 1934 | *[Badger's Green](/wiki/Badger%27s_Green_(1934_film) "Badger's Green (1934 film)")* | Adrian Brunel | First production credit of [Anthony Havelock-Allan](/wiki/Anthony_Havelock-Allan "Anthony Havelock-Allan") |
| 1934 | *[The Path of Glory](/wiki/The_Path_of_Glory "The Path of Glory")* | Dallas Bower | Exceptionally sophisticated and polished quota quickie |
| 1934 | *[To Be a Lady](/wiki/To_Be_a_Lady "To Be a Lady")* | George King | Sound film starring silent cinema star [Chili Bouchier](/wiki/Chili_Bouchier "Chili Bouchier") |
| 1935 | *[Murder at Monte Carlo](/wiki/Murder_at_Monte_Carlo "Murder at Monte Carlo")* | [Ralph Ince](/wiki/Ralph_Ince "Ralph Ince") | Screen debut of [Errol Flynn](/wiki/Errol_Flynn "Errol Flynn") |
| 1935 | *[The Price of a Song](/wiki/The_Price_of_a_Song "The Price of a Song")* | Michael Powell | One of Powell's most favourably reviewed quota quickies |
| 1935 | *[The Public Life of Henry the Ninth](/wiki/The_Public_Life_of_Henry_the_Ninth "The Public Life of Henry the Ninth")* | Bernard Mainwaring | First-ever [Hammer Films](/wiki/Hammer_Film_Productions "Hammer Film Productions") production |
| 1936 | *[Educated Evans](/wiki/Educated_Evans_(film) "Educated Evans (film)")* | [William Beaudine](/wiki/William_Beaudine "William Beaudine") | Considered the best of [Max Miller](/wiki/Max_Miller_(comedian) "Max Miller (comedian)")'s films |
| 1936 | *[The Man Behind the Mask](/wiki/The_Man_Behind_the_Mask "The Man Behind the Mask")* | Michael Powell | Powell's last quota quickie. A print of the American release, titled *Behind the Mask*, has been found, but it is a cut version of the original UK film. |
| 1936 | *[The Scarab Murder Case](/wiki/The_Scarab_Murder_Case_(film) "The Scarab Murder Case (film)")* | Michael Hankinson | The only [Philo Vance](/wiki/Philo_Vance "Philo Vance") film made in Britain |
| 1937 | *[The Vulture](/wiki/The_Vulture_(1937_film) "The Vulture (1937 film)")* | Ralph Ince | Last film directed by Ince before his death in a road accident |
| 1938 | *[The Viper](/wiki/The_Viper_(1938_film) "The Viper (1938 film)")* | [Roy William Neill](/wiki/Roy_William_Neill "Roy William Neill") | Sequel to *The Vulture* |
| 1939 | *[The Good Old Days](/wiki/The_Good_Old_Days_(film) "The Good Old Days (film)")* | Roy William Neill | The only Max Miller film with a period setting |
| 1939 | *[Murder Will Out](/wiki/Murder_Will_Out_(1939_film) "Murder Will Out (1939 film)")* | Roy William Neill | Playing in cinemas at outbreak of World War II |
| 1940 | *[Dr. O'Dowd](/wiki/Dr._O%27Dowd "Dr. O'Dowd")* | [Herbert Mason](/wiki/Herbert_Mason "Herbert Mason") | Irish-set drama, screen debut of [Peggy Cummins](/wiki/Peggy_Cummins "Peggy Cummins"). Enthusiastically reviewed in Ireland ("a film about Ireland with a difference...no animals in the living rooms of the homes.") |
| 1941 | *[This Man Is Dangerous](/wiki/This_Man_Is_Dangerous_(1941_film) "This Man Is Dangerous (1941 film)")* | [Lawrence Huntington](/wiki/Lawrence_Huntington "Lawrence Huntington") | The only missing [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason") film. Although it is said to have been shown on British television as recently as 1987, this is a false claim. Dubbed Italian copy traced.[[7]](#cite_note-BFI2017-7) |
| 1943 | *[Deadlock](/wiki/Deadlock_(1943_film) "Deadlock (1943 film)")* | Ronald Haines | Convoluted thriller with [John Slater](/wiki/John_Slater_(actor) "John Slater (actor)") in dual role as twins. It is now available on DVD.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) |
| 1943 | *[It's in the Bag](/wiki/It%27s_in_the_Bag_(1944_film) "It's in the Bag (1944 film)")* | Herbert Mason | Popular [Gert and Daisy](/wiki/Gert_and_Daisy "Gert and Daisy") slapstick comedy |
| 1944 | *[Kiss the Bride Goodbye](/wiki/Kiss_the_Bride_Goodbye "Kiss the Bride Goodbye")* | [Paul L. Stein](/wiki/Paul_L._Stein "Paul L. Stein") | Pre-stardom [Jean Simmons](/wiki/Jean_Simmons "Jean Simmons") role. The [Huntley Film Archives](/wiki/John_Huntley_(film_historian) "John Huntley (film historian)") states that it has "the whole film".[[8]](#cite_note-8) |
| 1944 | *[Welcome, Mr. Washington](/wiki/Welcome,_Mr._Washington "Welcome, Mr. Washington")* | [Leslie S. Hiscott](/wiki/Leslie_S._Hiscott "Leslie S. Hiscott") | American soldiers in an English village. Rediscovered c. 2015.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It was shown on the British TV channel [Talking Pictures TV](/wiki/Talking_Pictures_TV "Talking Pictures TV") on 13 October 2020.[[10]](#cite_note-10) |
| 1945 | *[Flight from Folly](/wiki/Flight_from_Folly "Flight from Folly")* | Herbert Mason | First starring screen role of stage star [Pat Kirkwood](/wiki/Pat_Kirkwood_(actress) "Pat Kirkwood (actress)") |
| 1945 | *[For You Alone](/wiki/For_You_Alone "For You Alone")* | [Geoffrey Faithfull](/wiki/Geoffrey_Faithfull "Geoffrey Faithfull") | Lavish wartime melodrama, a huge box-office hit. A 16mm safety print appears in the *UCLA* Film and Television Archive's online search.[[11]](#cite_note-11) |
| 1945 | *[The World Owes Me a Living](/wiki/The_World_Owes_Me_a_Living "The World Owes Me a Living")* | [Vernon Sewell](/wiki/Vernon_Sewell "Vernon Sewell") | Lost film from a re-evaluated director. The [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") possesses "nitrate material".[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) In 2020, the film was shown on [Talking Pictures TV](/wiki/Talking_Pictures_TV "Talking Pictures TV"). |
| 1948 | *[Bless 'Em All](/wiki/Bless_%27Em_All_(film) "Bless 'Em All (film)")* | [Robert Jordan Hill](/wiki/Robert_Jordan_Hill "Robert Jordan Hill") | Army comedy-musical, screen debut of [Max Bygraves](/wiki/Max_Bygraves "Max Bygraves"). A 2½-minute trailer survives, while a cut-down version titled *Be Kind Sergeant* turned up on eBay.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) |
| 1948 | *[But Not in Vain](/wiki/But_Not_in_Vain "But Not in Vain")* | [Edmond T. Gréville](/wiki/Edmond_T._Gr%C3%A9ville "Edmond T. Gréville") | Tense World War II drama by increasingly studied director |
| 1949 | *[The Golden Madonna](/wiki/The_Golden_Madonna "The Golden Madonna")* | [Ladislao Vajda](/wiki/Ladislao_Vajda "Ladislao Vajda") | Location-shot in Italy, starring [Phyllis Calvert](/wiki/Phyllis_Calvert "Phyllis Calvert") |
| 1950 | *[Double Confession](/wiki/Double_Confession "Double Confession")* | [Ken Annakin](/wiki/Ken_Annakin "Ken Annakin") | [Peter Lorre](/wiki/Peter_Lorre "Peter Lorre")'s only non-Hitchcock British film. A DVD was released but is no longer available.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) A 35mm print exists in an independent archive in the UK. |
| 1952 | *[Hammer the Toff](/wiki/Hammer_the_Toff "Hammer the Toff")* | [Maclean Rogers](/wiki/Maclean_Rogers "Maclean Rogers") | Two films based on the [John Creasey](/wiki/John_Creasey "John Creasey") character [The Toff](/wiki/The_Toff "The Toff"). *Salute the Toff* was released on DVD in November 2013 and *Hammer the Toff* in March 2016. |
| 1952 | *[Salute the Toff](/wiki/Salute_the_Toff "Salute the Toff")* |
| 1953 | *[Small Town Story](/wiki/Small_Town_Story_(film) "Small Town Story (film)")* | [Montgomery Tully](/wiki/Montgomery_Tully "Montgomery Tully") | Football thriller with appearances by [Denis Compton](/wiki/Denis_Compton "Denis Compton") and the [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") and [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") football teams. Starring [Donald Houston](/wiki/Donald_Houston "Donald Houston") and [Susan Shaw](/wiki/Susan_Shaw "Susan Shaw"). Has now been found, restored and released on DVD.[[12]](#cite_note-12) |
| 1953 | *[Three Steps in the Dark](/wiki/Three_Steps_in_the_Dark "Three Steps in the Dark")* | [Daniel Birt](/wiki/Daniel_Birt "Daniel Birt") | Murder mystery starring [Greta Gynt](/wiki/Greta_Gynt "Greta Gynt"). It is in the collection of the National Film and Sound Archive in Australia.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) |
| 1954 | *[The Diamond](/wiki/The_Diamond_(film) "The Diamond (film)")* | Montgomery Tully | The first British [3D film](/wiki/3D_film "3D film"). According to BFI, however, it was shown only once in 3D, on 13 September 2006 in Hollywood.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first nearly five minutes can also be viewed on YouTube.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The complete film is available on Amazon Prime. |
| 1957 | *[Alive on Saturday](/wiki/Alive_on_Saturday "Alive on Saturday")* | [Alfred Travers](/wiki/Alfred_Travers "Alfred Travers") | Stars [Guy Middleton](/wiki/Guy_Middleton "Guy Middleton") and [Patricia Owens](/wiki/Patricia_Owens "Patricia Owens"). 'The BFI's Stills, Posters and Design collections holds two stills.'[[15]](#cite_note-15) |
| 1957 | *[Second Fiddle](/wiki/Second_Fiddle_(1957_film) "Second Fiddle (1957 film)")* | Maurice Elvey | Elvey's last film, it is now available on DVD.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) |
| 1962 | *[Crosstrap](/wiki/Crosstrap "Crosstrap")* | [Robert Hartford-Davis](/wiki/Robert_Hartford-Davis "Robert Hartford-Davis") | Directorial debut, reportedly with exceptionally graphic violence for its time. The BFI reported that a black and white negative print of the film was discovered in the early 2010s and digitally scanned. It is now available for screening on the BFI player website,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and has been shown several times on [Talking Pictures TV](/wiki/Talking_Pictures_TV "Talking Pictures TV"). |
| 1963 | *[Farewell Performance](/wiki/Farewell_Performance "Farewell Performance")* | [Robert Tronson](/wiki/Robert_Tronson "Robert Tronson") | Murder mystery set in the pop world, with performances from [Joe Meek](/wiki/Joe_Meek "Joe Meek") acts including [The Tornados](/wiki/The_Tornados "The Tornados") and [Heinz](/wiki/Heinz_(singer) "Heinz (singer)") |
| 1969 | *[The Promise](/wiki/The_Promise_(1969_film) "The Promise (1969 film)")* | Michael Hayes | The first time Russian playwright [Aleksei Arbuzov](/wiki/Aleksei_Arbuzov "Aleksei Arbuzov") allowed any of his works to be filmed. Stars [Ian McKellen](/wiki/Ian_McKellen "Ian McKellen"). |
| 1971 | *[Nobody Ordered Love](/wiki/Nobody_Ordered_Love "Nobody Ordered Love")* | Robert Hartford-Davis | Following poor promotion and a critical panning, Hartford-Davis reportedly took back all prints and ordered them to be destroyed after his death. |
| 1972 | *[The Cherry Picker](/wiki/The_Cherry_Picker "The Cherry Picker")* | [Peter Curran](/wiki/Peter_Curran_(director) "Peter Curran (director)") | Mild [sexploitation](/wiki/Sexploitation_film "Sexploitation film") comedy with cast including [Lulu](/wiki/Lulu_(singer) "Lulu (singer)"), [Spike Milligan](/wiki/Spike_Milligan "Spike Milligan"), and [Terry-Thomas](/wiki/Terry-Thomas "Terry-Thomas"). Believed to be still in private circulation via inferior quality bootleg copies, but original prints and negatives are missing. |
| 1983 | *[Where Is Parsifal?](/wiki/Where_Is_Parsifal%3F "Where Is Parsifal?")* | Henri Helman | Cast includes [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles"), [Tony Curtis](/wiki/Tony_Curtis "Tony Curtis"), and [Peter Lawford](/wiki/Peter_Lawford "Peter Lawford"). Shown at [1984 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1984_Cannes_Film_Festival "1984 Cannes Film Festival") but withdrawn before scheduled UK release. Never publicly available in UK or US, the original English-language sources are missing. Director Helman donated "his personal 35mm print, with French subtitles" to the British Film Institute.[[2]](#cite_note-BFI2012-2) |
